evals.prompt: Run a competitive analysis for my SaaS project management tool. Main competitors are Asana, Monday.com, and Linear. We're targeting small engineering teams (5-20 people). I need the full Porter's 5 Forces, a competitor matrix, and a positioning map — want to take this to a board meeting.
evals.expected_output: Full competitive analysis with all five Porter's forces rated (HIGH/MODERATE/LOW), a competitor matrix comparing Asana/Monday.com/Linear, a Mermaid quadrantChart positioning map, and strategic recommendations tailored to small engineering teams and board presentation context.

deterministic_checks.description: Porter's force 1: Competitive Rivalry is present
Porter's force 2: Supplier Power is present
Porter's force 3: Buyer Power is present
Porter's force 4: Threat of Substitution is present
Porter's force 5: Threat of New Entry is present
At least one force carries an explicit rating
Required Competitive Matrix section is present
Positioning map rendered as Mermaid quadrantChart (2+ named competitors satisfy the condition)
Required Strategic Recommendations section is present
Named competitor Asana appears in the analysis
Named competitor Linear appears in the analysis
Trend indicators are applied to at least one competitor
No internal MIF URN leakage in output
No internal finding-ID leakage in output

expectations.description: Each force carries an industry-specific rationale, not boilerplate
expectations.criteria: The Porter's analysis mentions SaaS project management specifics (e.g., integrations, freemium models, switching costs from workflow lock-in) rather than generic force descriptions

expectations.description: Analysis is tailored to small engineering teams
expectations.criteria: The competitor matrix and recommendations reflect the 5-20 person engineering team context — not generic enterprise or marketing-team positioning
Given the board meeting context, the output includes a clear strategic takeaway or executive-facing summary, not just raw data tables

expectations.description: Confidence tiers or source notes are present
expectations.criteria: At least some claims are labelled with a confidence tier (High/Medium/Low) or source reference, not treated as unqualified facts

evals.prompt: I'm launching an AI-native code review tool — there's nothing quite like it yet. Can you do a competitive analysis? I know traditional code review tools exist (GitHub PR review, Gerrit) and some AI coding assistants (Copilot, Cursor) but nobody is specifically doing AI-native code review as a standalone product.
evals.expected_output: Emerging-category competitive analysis that identifies substitutes and indirect/potential competitors, applies an adapted Porter's framework (rivalry LOW, new entry HIGH, substitution VERY HIGH), addresses first-mover dynamics, and recommends on category definition — without fabricating direct competitors that do not exist.

deterministic_checks.description: All five forces present: Competitive Rivalry
All five forces present: Supplier Power
All five forces present: Buyer Power
All five forces present: Threat of Substitution
All five forces present: Threat of New Entry
Required Competitive Matrix section present
Required Strategic Recommendations section present
Analysis identifies substitutes or indirect competitors as required for emerging categories
Trend indicators applied to at least one competitor or substitute
No internal MIF URN leakage
No internal finding-ID leakage

expectations.description: Porter's forces are adapted for an emerging/new-category market
expectations.criteria: The analysis reflects that rivalry is low (few direct competitors), threat of new entry is high (well-funded players could pivot), and threat of substitution is very high (existing tools serve the need today) — not just copy-paste generic ratings

expectations.description: First-mover and category-creation dynamics are addressed
expectations.criteria: The output discusses advantages and risks of creating a new category (e.g., education burden, potential for high switching costs if the category sticks, risk of fast follower from GitHub/JetBrains)

expectations.description: Analysis does not fabricate nonexistent direct competitors
expectations.criteria: The skill does not invent fictional AI-native code review companies. It frames GitHub PR review, Copilot, Cursor, and Gerrit as indirect or substitute competitors with honest labels

evals.prompt: Hey can you do a competitive analysis for the cloud data warehouse space? Snowflake, BigQuery, and Redshift are the big three. Databricks is becoming a factor. Looking for help deciding whether to build on top of one of these or compete directly.
evals.expected_output: Full Porter's 5 Forces analysis for cloud data warehouses, competitor matrix covering Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift/Databricks, positioning map (Mermaid quadrant), and strategic recommendations that directly address the build-on-top vs. compete-directly decision context.

deterministic_checks.description: Force 1 present
Force 2 present
Force 3 present
Force 4 present
Force 5 present
Explicit force ratings present
Competitor Snowflake in analysis
Competitor BigQuery in analysis
Competitor Databricks in analysis
Required Competitive Matrix section
Positioning map as Mermaid quadrantChart (4 named competitors satisfy the 2+ condition)
Strategic Recommendations section present
Trend indicators present
No MIF URN leakage
No finding-ID leakage

expectations.description: Build-on-top vs. compete-directly decision is addressed in recommendations
expectations.criteria: The Strategic Recommendations section explicitly weighs the two strategic options the user named — build on one of the platforms vs. compete directly — with specific reasoning rather than ignoring the framing

expectations.description: Positioning map dimensions are relevant to the cloud warehouse space
expectations.criteria: The quadrantChart axes reflect meaningful warehouse-specific dimensions (e.g., query performance vs. cost, open table format support vs. vendor lock-in, data engineering focus vs. BI focus) rather than generic dimensions

expectations.description: Competitor matrix contains substantive dimension values
expectations.criteria: The Competitive Matrix rows include pricing model (consumption-based, seat-based, etc.), strengths, and weaknesses specific to each platform — not placeholder text like 'List' or 'TBD'

evals.prompt: I want a competitive analysis but I haven't decided what market yet. Just give me something useful.
evals.expected_output: Skill asks clarifying questions about the market/industry, product, known competitors, goal, and target segment rather than fabricating an analysis. Does NOT produce a Porter's 5 Forces table or competitor matrix for an unknown market.

deterministic_checks.description: Skill asks at least one clarifying question about scope
No MIF URN leakage
No finding-ID leakage

expectations.description: Skill does not fabricate analysis for an unspecified market
expectations.criteria: The skill does NOT produce a Porter's 5 Forces analysis, competitor matrix, or positioning map — it acknowledges the missing context and asks clarifying questions before proceeding

expectations.description: Clarifying questions cover the required dimensions
expectations.criteria: The questions ask for at least two of: market/industry, the user's product, known competitors, strategic goal, and target segment — as specified in the SKILL.md Handling Incomplete Prompts section

expectations.description: Response is helpful rather than a refusal
expectations.criteria: The skill describes what it will deliver once context is provided (e.g., mentions Porter's 5 Forces, competitor matrix, positioning map as expected outputs) rather than just saying it cannot help
